### Senior AI ML Engineer(Gen AI) - [Elevance Health](https://www.linkedin.com/company/elevance-health) (Current)
...
Currently working as a Senior AI/ML Engineer (Gen AI) at Elevance Health, one of the largest health insurance companies in the United States, focused on building an AI-powered Clinical Decision Intelligence platform that helps healthcare professionals make smarter, faster decisions using advanced Generative AI and real clinical data. In this role, I architect and maintain a microservices-based, event-driven platform powered by GPT-4, Llama 3, and Claude through Azure OpenAI, integrating Python, FastAPI, Kubernetes, gRPC, and Kafka to handle large volumes of clinical data in real time. I build and manage robust data pipelines that ingest information from SQL databases, REST APIs, Blob Storage, and Kafka streams, transforming it into clean, AI-ready formats using PySpark and Azure Data Factory. A key focus of my work is designing and refining RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipelines, where AI models retrieve relevant context from enterprise knowledge bases before generating accurate, grounded responses. I manage vector databases including Pinecone, FAISS, and Azure AI Search, and apply advanced prompting techniques like Chain-of-Thought and ReAct to continuously improve the quality of AI outputs in clinical settings. On the deployment and operations side, I containerize applications with Docker, orchestrate them on Kubernetes with auto-scaling, and automate CI/CD pipelines using GitHub Actions and Azure DevOps. Model quality is tracked through MLflow, RAGAS, and TruLens, while system observability is maintained via Prometheus, Grafana, and Azure Monitor, ensuring the platform stays reliable, scalable, and enterprise-grade.

Everyone wants to hire AI/ML engineers. Most companies don't know what they actually need. Here's a guide.
 
 I've reviewed 20+ AI/ML Engineer JDs in the last 3 months. The requirements range from reasonable to completely divorced from reality.
 
 DATA SCIENTIST:
 Statistical modelling, ML algorithms, Jupyter notebooks. Strong Python — Pandas, Scikit-learn, Matplotlib. Translates insights to business. NOT usually responsible for productionising their own models.
 
 ML ENGINEER:
 Takes Data Scientist models and makes them production-ready. MLOps — model deployment, monitoring, retraining pipelines. Tools: MLflow, Kubeflow, SageMaker, Vertex AI. Strong software engineering background required.
 
 AI/LLM ENGINEER — new but growing fast:
 Prompt engineering, RAG architectures, LangChain, LlamaIndex. Fine-tuning open-source LLMs — LLaMA, Mistral. API integration of OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini. This role barely existed 18 months ago.
 
 Most common client mistake: asking for all three in one role.
 
 Happy to help companies define what they actually need before opening a JD. DM me. #AIEngineering #MachineLearning #MLOps #TechRecruitment #DataScience #LLM #GenerativeAI #AIJobs

I’m a data scientist who actually enjoys the messy intersection of math, risk, and real-world decision-making. I don't just chase high accuracy scores on clean datasets; I build systems that help organizations navigate uncertainty. Most of my work sits in healthcare and finance, where the data is messy, the stakes are high, and getting it slightly wrong can be expensive. At Elevance Health, I focus on spotting early cost and utilization signals across claims and pharmacy data. A lot of that work is about understanding how things change over time, what normal really looks like, and when a shift is worth paying attention to. The end goal is always the same: build systems leaders can trust, not just models that look good in a notebook. Before that, I was part of Indiana University’s Building a Mind Lab, working on mental-health prediction using multimodal data. We were combining physiological signals, survey responses, and behavioral patterns, which meant spending a lot of time on signal quality, feature dynamics, and model stability. It was very hands-on, from designing secure ML pipelines to validating models and shipping them into real research workflows. Earlier in my career, I worked on fraud, anomaly detection, and financial risk problems. That’s where I picked up a strong quantitative mindset. Instead of hard rules or thresholds, the focus was on expected behavior, variance, and how to measure deviation in a way that holds up when the data shifts. Across roles, I usually find myself at the intersection of quantitative modeling, risk and anomaly detection, forecasting and early-signal discovery, and applied AI systems that run in production. I’m most comfortable in spaces where the answers aren’t obvious, and assumptions actually matter. If the work involves making decisions under uncertainty and building AI that people genuinely rely on, that’s usually where I do my best work.
